item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article could not be sourced, and therefore is to be assumed as non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that there's a lot the author could've done with something that enforces the idea of a crime, which is pretty arbitrary, with disproportionate severity. I was ready to no-vote for a so-so Series 2 execution with some missed potential, but then I got to the test log. The test log was goofy and awful enough for me to downvote. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting concept, but perhaps more can be added? A testing log would be beneficial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Containment is a bit lax, yet overly-specific in all the wrong ways.

Why a 6m2 room and then a 10m no-go radius? It would make more sense to have either a room that encompasses the whole area of effect or else don't specify the room dimensions and just establish the red line perimeter.

Requiring guards that have "…not yet killed in a combat situation." implies that the shadow cops care about circumstances. But that isn't supported by the text of the effect.
In any case, the guard requirement should be stricter. Because as is one of them may have eaten a grape at the grocery store, ending up eyeless and (more important to the Foundation) combat-ineffective.

These are medium-large nitpicks. They do detract from my enjoyment, but I still enjoyed it and upvoted accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given that the personnel involved can identify the silhouette of a guard of a specific casino, why can't they tell what kind of Special Weapons And Tactics team a shadow belongs to? SWAT isn't even an official classification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just read this one for the first time, and going through the comments, I'm so sad that none of the advice was ever applied. It is rated +86 as of this comment, so enough people clearly like it as it is, but damn…I feel like it has such great potential just within reach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A finger-severing pigeon helicopter with a 10-meter radius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If that pigeon flies out we’re all screwed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but it could easily be so much better. I think it would be more interesting if there was a bit more diversity with the law enforcement (the Roman soldier was a nice touch), and if they only reacted to crimes of that particular time period. In addition to this, the crimes listed seem pretty limited, there's a lot of serious crimes that aren't murder, assault, or burglary. As was mentioned higher up, what happens to a rapist? Does the punishment change if the victims of the crime were "worse" i.e. a person who killed an infant child rather than a person who killed a drunk guy who was attacking him.

Everything just seems so vague, and while I like the idea enough to upvote, it would be good if someone went in added some things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this needs to be more secretive. The final note "Mass-Murder: Non-fatal removal of arms, legs, ears, tongue and eyes. Subject was then…" does not have enough [REDACTED] parts to it.

The rest is pretty nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And if you're a rapist…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember this being one of the first SCPs that I ever read. Ah, memories….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, though, to echo previous comments, a test log would be nice, and clarification would help - for example, I know you implied it by requiring security guards who haven't killed, but would killing in self-defense count? How does it react to law enforcement? Minor quibbles, but it's pretty solid, otherwis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The punishments feel too severe. I know that's kinda the point, but seriously, cutting off fingers for littering in the past six months? I imagine that just beating the snot out of someone with a billy club/sword pommel/riot shield/fist would be enough for such a minor crime. As it is, the ridiculousness of the first few punishments makes the severity of the last ones feel reduced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, you're right. It's the shadow that does it.

That said, I find the mental image of a pigeon having a full-sized humanoid shadow (and an airplane have a relatively tiny shadow!) to be humorous in a weird way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I'm reading this right, it's the pigeons humanoid shadow that actually does it. I could be wrong though, the process isn't entirely clear to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How, exactly, does a pigeon cut off your hands?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a bit vague on what the 'crimes' actually are, for one. For another, it sort of verges on a Thing Wot Kills You.

That said, you've got an interesting thing going with the picture and the shadows. -1 for now, but give it a rewrite and some more detail and that should change to a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The improbability of being able to state definitively which PD a shadow is representing really sabotages suspension of disbelief and makes it hard to take the rest of the article seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. That said, I feel there are a few issues that could be addressed.

  • Why are the containment rooms lit? A dark room throws no shadows. Or can the SCP-1002-XA instance manifest even in the dark?
  • If the SCP-1002-XA instance manifests when a target enters a 10m perimeter of SCP-1002-X then why is the smaller containment room 6mx6m? Surely the Foundation has the resources to make the room 12mx12m and contain the items in the central 2mx2m.
  • If a 10m breach of perimeter is required for SCP-1002-XA to manifest, and it pursues a target, how is it catching people? Remarkable agility is one thing, but if I see a dark shadowy humanoid manifest 9.5m in front of me, the first thing I'm going to do is take a step back. Maybe the SCP-1002-XA instances need to warp to individual?

I also second the earlier suggestion of rewriting the crimes/punishments as a test log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was it just me that read "high levels of agility" and imagined an assassin's creed type chase?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some of the identities of the shadows seem improbable. A roman soldier has a pretty recognizable profile, but how can the Foundation tell that number 5 is from the ██████ Casino? Unless they have uniforms that are very distinctive in silhouette, it would be impossible to tell. Same thing with the policemen. We couldn't see badges or logos, so how do we know they're from specific cities?

Also, upon rereading I noticed that number 6 is, in fact, an airplane. I feel silly for not noticing sooner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I definitely like the idea, but the phrasing of "tied to SCP-1002" was confusing until about the third time I read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is solid and you have a good picture. For that, I upvoted it.

That being said, the writing style needs a lot of work. Some of the phrasing is a bit awkward, and the tone feels off as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, no, that's not how it works.

"AAUUGH!! My arms! You cut off my arms! AAAH! The pain, the pain. Wait, what are you doing? Are you… cutting the hands off my detached arms? ARRRRGGHHHHH!!!!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's… Not bad. It's written somewhat well, and I do have to admit the punishments made me cringe a bit, due to an overactive imagination. So it definitely has the creepiness factor there.

The only thing that strikes me as off, is the definition of the crimes the shadows react to. Mostly it has to do with the fact that each shadow is associated with a different era, and possibly a location within that era.

It might have been better to write it out describing that the shadows reacted to what would have been considered crimes of the eras and locations that they are depicted as coming from. Granted, you covered the general cases which would have been considered crimes in most, if not all societies, regardless of era and location.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feels like overkill to me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Non-fatal removal of arms, legs, hands, fingers,

I'd pay good money to see how to remove someone's arms without removing his hands and fingers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was expecting the shadow to do something as a shadow, and was a bit disappointed when it didn't. If you're not attached to the "becomes physical" part of your concept, a suggestion: the shadow remains a shadow, and attacks the shadow of the person who committed the crime, without the person suffering any injuries, or even feeling anything. The person's shadow will become out of sync with the person, looking and acting as if the person had suffered the injuries:

  • If the shadow suffered a leg injury, it will limp even though the person doesn't. If the injury is minor enough, the limp will eventually go away.
  • If the shadow had a finger cut off, the person's finger will be permanently shadow-less.
  • If the shadow was killed, the person will permanently have no shadow whatsoever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Committed a crime" seems pretty vague. After all, laws change through different years and civilizations. Do all of the shadows, though they are from differing time periods, respond in kind to the same set of crimes? What constitutes a crime and what does not?

The list of crimes and punishments at the end also strikes me as imprecise. Maybe a more in-depth testing log?

I like the base concept tho.
